title: Fix Restart Issue with Pasta Networking Driver
body:
<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

For more detailed information, please review our contributing guidelines:
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests
-->

#### Checklist

Ensure you have completed the following checklist for your pull request to be reviewed:
<!-- Use [x] to mark as done, or click the checkbox after opening PR -->

- [x] Certify you wrote the patch or otherwise have the right to pass it on as an open-source patch by signing all
commits. (`git commit -s`). (If needed, use `git commit -s --amend`).  The author email must match
the sign-off email address. See [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#sign-your-prs)
for more information.
- [x] Referenced issues using `Fixes: #00000` in commit message (if applicable)
- [x] [Tests](https://github.com/containers/podman/tree/main/test#readme) have been added/updated (or no tests are needed)
- [x] [Documentation](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/README.md) has been updated (or no documentation changes are needed)
- [x] All commits pass `make validatepr` (format/lint checks)
- [x] [Release note](https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/guide/release-notes.md) entered in the section below (or `None` if no user-facing changes)

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
Write `None` if there are no user-facing changes, otherwise enter your release note below.
Include "action required" if users need to take action when upgrading.
-->

```release-note
None
```

When a container using pasta networking is restarted, the pasta process may not exit immediately when its network namespace is deleted. This causes the new pasta instance to fail binding to the same ports with "address already in use" errors.

This PR adds explicit termination of the pasta process during network teardown:
1. Search /proc for pasta processes matching the container's netns path
2. Send SIGTERM and wait up to 1 second for graceful exit
3. Fall back to SIGKILL if the process doesn't respond

The implementation includes safeguards against false positives (only matches executables named exactly pasta or paths ending in /pasta) and handles race conditions gracefully.

  Fixes: #23737

comments.body:
Failing workflows appear unrelated to networking code/tests implemented in the commits:

# Int podman fedora-43 root host
Failing in shared layer testing:
```[+0829s]   [FAIL] Podman rmi [It] podman image rm - concurrent with shared layers
[+0829s]   /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman/test/e2e/common_test.go:1528
```
Test comments suggest the test is not reliable:
```
        It("podman image rm - concurrent with shared layers", func() {
                // #6510 has shown a fairly simple reproducer to force storage
                // errors during parallel image removal.  Since it's subject to
                // a race, we may not hit the condition a 100 percent of times
                // but ocal reproducers hit it all the time.
```

# int podman rawhide root host
Failing in log coloring:
```
[+0883s] Summarizing 1 Failure:
[+0883s]   [FAIL] Podman logs [It] podman pod logs with different colors
[+0883s]   /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman/test/e2e/logs_test.go:693
```

# testing-farm:fedora-rawhide-x86_64:podman-fedora 
Test duration is capped at 30m, test failed at 30m mark:
```
ok 780 podman --ssh test # skip only applicable on podman-remote
ok 781 [950] podman preexec hook

Maximum test time '30m' exceeded.
Adjust the test 'duration' attribute if necessary.
https://tmt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/spec/tests.html#duration
```

Thank you for your pull request submission.  I have restarted the failed tests.  Given the topic of this PR, I really want @mheon and @Luap99 to review it prior to merging and they both happen to be on PTO this week.  I'll walk through it as well but wont give a very deep dive.
@sbrivio-rh mind also reviewing?

Sorry, I wasn't aware of the issue at all, I would have looked into it earlier (I'm the original author of Podman's pasta integration).

> When a container using pasta networking is restarted, the pasta process may not exit immediately when its network namespace is deleted.

@drchristensen do you happen to know why? Isn't that something we should fix instead?

In general, pasta's interfaces were implemented with typical integrations (Podman, moby/rootlesskit) in mind, trying to keep them as simple as possible. This adds substantial complexity for a behaviour that, I guess, wasn't intended.

Another remark from a first quick read:

> Search /proc for pasta processes matching the container's netns path

You don't really need to, pasta can save its PID file somewhere if you want. But again, Podman's integration doesn't ask for that because it wasn't needed. If it became needed all of a sudden, it can be added. The option is `--pid` / `-P` (I still hope we don't actually need it).

> Sorry, I wasn't aware of the issue at all, I would have looked into it earlier (I'm the original author of Podman's pasta integration).
> 
> > When a container using pasta networking is restarted, the pasta process may not exit immediately when its network namespace is deleted.
> 
> @drchristensen do you happen to know why? Isn't that something we should fix instead?

Pasta implementation in vendor/go.podman.io/common/libnetwork/pasta/pasta_linux.go:55-56 states:
"Note that there is no need for any special cleanup logic, the pasta process will automatically exit when the netns path is deleted."

But pasta doesn't exit synchronously when the network namespace is deleted. Instead, it detects netns deletion using:
  1. inotify — watching for the netns file to be unmounted
  2. Polling fallback — checking every 1 second if the netns still exists

This may lead to a race condition when podman restarts a container:
  1. Deletes the old netns
  2. Creates a new netns
  3. Starts a new pasta process

The old pasta process may still be running during step 3 because it hasn't yet detected the netns deletion. Since it's still holding the port binding, the new pasta fails with "address already in use."

The podman change is a reasonable defensive code implementation. Even if pasta improves, having explicit cleanup as a fallback is sensible.

I don't have lot of experience with this part of code, but can a single netns and single pasta process be shared by multiple containers? I'm thinking about a situation where we kill pasta process for second container while stopping the first container.

Thanks for your contribution. I'm not an expert here. I've just added some ideas and opinions.

In general, I think adding `-p` to get the pid of pasta would be less error-prone and make everything much easier, but even without that, your implementation improves the current situation.

> I don't have lot of experience with this part of code, but can a single netns and single pasta process be shared by multiple containers?

In a near future, once we finish implementing https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=140, that will be possible, see also https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/contrib/design-docs/podman6hld.md#pasta.

> I'm thinking about a situation where we kill pasta process for second container while stopping the first container.

Yes, I think that would be a problem.

> In general, I think adding `-p` to get the pid of pasta would be less error-prone and make everything much easier

Right, if really needed, that would be as designed. It's what libvirt uses for passt(1) by the way, in case an early termination (e.g. hypervisor didn't start) is needed.

> but even without that, your implementation improves the current situation.

I'm not sure if it's really an improvement in the sense that this might be hiding missing rules in the SELinux policy or whatever is preventing pasta from setting up an inotify watch on the namespace entry.

We had issues recently reported in that sense and we still have at least one known issue pending, see https://issues.redhat.com/browse/RHEL-136495.

But I'm currently swamped with other stuff so I can only get back to this in a couple of days.

> But I'm currently swamped with other stuff so I can only get back to this in a couple of days.

By the way, if somebody is trying to help out with this kind of issue (including https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23737), I could really use some help trying to figure out the remaining bits of https://issues.redhat.com/browse/RHEL-136495, and possibly related issues (perhaps including some of the current pending Fedora bugs, https://bugzilla.redhat.com/buglist.cgi?cmdtype=runnamed&list_id=13644708&namedcmd=passt&sharer_id=410109, which are all about SELinux).

For example, are SELinux (or AppArmor) denials reported in cases where container restarts fail? Is there a `watch` rule still missing in the policy?

@sbrivio-rh There are no policy problems around that here I believe

There is nothing wrong with pasta's it exist on its own, the issue is that pasta exit detection races again podman spawning a new pasta process on container start with the same ports. That is fundamentally not solvable by pasta, podman must wait for pasta to exit when stopping the container to avoid this.

By the way I'm currently experimenting with some different approaches, give me a bit. Given https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23737#issuecomment-3764078193 and @Luap99's reports, I'm now simply making pasta wait before exiting in order to investigate other ideas.

Just as a quick remark: even using the PID file to send SIGKILL, or just SIGTERM, might be problematic if you have pending TCP connections that should be closed properly. Using it to wait for pasta to terminate looks much safer, but I'm still hopeful we can avoid the complexity (albeit not much) associated with it.

interestingly enough I did try with a locally build pasta from HEAD and could no longer reproduce

I was however able to reproduce when building the latest tag 2026_01_17.81c97f6, so it seem the latest code changes in pasta fixed it already. Still no guarantee but I no longer observe any failure locally which is a huge improvement.
git bisect points me to b223bec48213060304c09882ce5b3055b15b7e07 as commit which fixes it, I can reproduce on the previous commit but not with that one.

So that said we still would like a guarantee in podman that pasta exited so I say we still need to add the pid argument and wait for pasta to really exit before continuing with cleanup.

> Let me go ahead and make a pasta (passt) release from the current HEAD, so that we have meanwhile a workaround that should always avoid the problem, in practice

This is now included in passt [2026_01_20.386b5f5](https://archives.passt.top/passt-user/20260121055331.64707ef8@elisabeth/).

Sorry, it took me a bit to explore a few possible alternatives here. First off, I still had to follow up with:

> > @drchristensen do you happen to know why? Isn't that something we should fix instead?
> 
> Pasta implementation in vendor/go.podman.io/common/libnetwork/pasta/pasta_linux.go:55-56 states: "Note that there is no need for any special cleanup logic, the pasta process will automatically exit when the netns path is deleted."
> 
> But pasta doesn't exit synchronously when the network namespace is deleted. Instead, it detects netns deletion using:
> 
> 1. inotify — watching for the netns file to be unmounted
> 2. Polling fallback — checking every 1 second if the netns still exists

Right, I implemented that. :wink: But I thought 1. would be order of magnitudes faster than Podman trying to restart networking for a container. Side note: there's no inotify event when the reference is unmounted. The entry is deleted and that's what pasta reacts to.

From further tests and reports, though, it became clear that the inotify mechanism can actually take long enough to cause that race.

I originally thought the timer fallback (intended for filesystems not supporting inotify) was needed to reproduce this, and I wanted to investigate if we switched to it because of some other issue. But that's not the case.

> The podman change is a reasonable defensive code implementation. Even if pasta improves, having explicit cleanup as a fallback is sensible.

Note that SIGTERM (pasta doesn't handle it) and SIGKILL (in any case) will interrupt system calls, so it's not desirable to use them as a routine mechanism, because they will abruptly interrupt reads and writes (in either direction), potentially leading to inconsistencies in transfers (e.g. file transfers). Letting pasta terminate cleanly is definitely desirable.

I think we have pretty much two options here:

1. before we consider the network namespace cleaned up, wait until pasta's interface disappears from the target namespace, signalling that pasta already exited. The kernel removes the interface as soon as pasta closes the corresponding descriptor, and we can detect that by looking into the `net/dev` procfs entry corresponding to any process in the namespace (for example conmon's child process). To avoid a further race, this needs a small change in pasta so that inbound listening sockets are closed (and ports unbound) before the tap descriptor is closed.

    * Pros:
      - no need to track the PID file, no need to clean it up, no need to adjust LSM policies
      - should we ever have multiple instances of pasta for a given namespace, it will still work
    * Cons:
      - a bit clunky, requires polling
      - no chance to forcibly terminate pasta after a given timeout is elapsed

2. get a pidfd using pidfd_open() on the PID written by pasta with `-p` and use that as Paul suggested to monitor the pasta process, and eventually terminate it after a given timeout. The descriptor would be pollable and there's already a corresponding implementation in Podman, so this part would be rather elegant. There would still be a small, albeit largely theoretical race: if pasta terminates after writing the PID file but before Podman fetches the matching pidfd, the PID file might now correspond to another process. We can't use the pidfd functionality from clone3() as pasta forks to background once it's ready.

    * Pros:
      - elegant, using interfaces exactly as intended
      - possibility of implementing a timeout
    * Cons:
      - we still have a small race (not really a practical issue though, because pasta won't actually exit early)
      - need to clean up PID and adjust LSM policies to allow this kind of interaction

Overall, I would have a small preference for 1., but it's definitely not a strong preference, and 2. is appealing as well in many ways.

I'll finish up my review in a bit, but I won't spend time myself on this right now, as the problem should now be entirely theoretical, with the current version of pasta. Needless to say, feel free to pick one and adjust the current proposal, though.

> Note that SIGTERM (pasta doesn't handle it) and SIGKILL (in any case) will interrupt system calls, so it's not desirable to use them as a routine mechanism, because they will abruptly interrupt reads and writes (in either direction), potentially leading to inconsistencies in transfers (e.g. file transfers). Letting pasta terminate cleanly is definitely desirable.

Only tangentially related, but is this an argument for always setting `SO_LINGER` (with 0 timeout) on our sockets?  I believe that would mean that if pasta is killed the peer would get a reset, rather than a misleading clean close.

> The kernel removes the interface as soon as pasta closes the corresponding descriptor, and we can detect that by looking into the net/dev procfs entry corresponding to any process in the namespace (for example conmon's child process).

There is no pid besides pasta left in the netns at this point. When we do call netns cleanup it is because the container exited on its own or was killed before, obviously we don't want to remove networking from the container process when they are still running.

The only way that would work is for the cleanup logic to open the netns again in podman and then monitor `/proc/thread-self/net/dev`  I suppose, not nice. Also practically speaking there is no reliable for us to know which interface is pasta's a container with CAP_NET_ADMIN could have created their own interfaces, or maybe some oci hook adding other interfaces and so on so blindly assuming there will only be lo back cannot work in all cases.
On the end end I guess you don't need to poll that file regardless, AFAIK you can monitor the netlink events so it could listen to that without active polling, i.e. compare ip monitor.

So that said I would prefer solution 2.


> need to clean up PID and adjust LSM policies to allow this kind of interaction

The one thing I thought here that could make sense is if pasta writes the pid to stdout since podman already reads and buffers it anyway. LSM wise that would not need changes, or as I mentioned before maybe passing an pipe instead of a file should work too, I guess that might still need some LSM rules but it would mean we would not need to hard code any allowed paths into LSM policies which I would consider a win.

> we still have a small race (not really a practical issue though, because pasta won't actually exit early)

Technically we could make this 100% race free if pasta uses clone3() to get the pidfd of the child (or well just do normal fork and call pidfd_open as long as you do not reap the child process before that we know the pid cannot be reused) and then use name_to_handle_at() to serialize to a unique identifier. Podman can then store this and open it again using open_by_handle_at(), see https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/pidhandle/pidhandle_linux.go

That works since kernel v6.14 AFAIK so we would still need the normal (non race free) pid parsing for older versions of course but just highlighting this can be made totally race free.
A friendly reminder that this PR had no activity for 30 days.
@drchristensen, any updates on this?
